retrieved_docs.title: Nonorganic Vision Loss -- Differential Diagnosis -- Commonly Misdiagnosed Organic Ocular Diseases
retrieved_docs.content: Big Blind Spot Syndrome: Also known as enlarged blind spot syndrome, this condition is marked by an idiopathic enlargement of the physiologic blind spot, commonly associated with photopsia, scotomas, and visual field defects. The optic nerve typically appears normal, which may lead to a misdiagnosis of functional visual loss. [45] The pathophysiology of this syndrome is unclear, and treatment focuses on monitoring for the development of associated conditions, such as multiple evanescent white dot syndrome (MEWDS) or acute idiopathic blind spot enlargement (AIBSE). [46] [47] Big blind spot syndrome may also be a paraneoplastic phenomenon. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypertrophy of Retinal Pigment Epithelium -- History and Physical -- Solitary or Unifocal Congenital Hypertrophy of the Retinal Pigmented Epithelium
retrieved_docs.content: The mean largest basal diameter of solitary CHRPE is approximately 4.7 mm (median 4.5 mm). [2] However, lesions can vary in size, ranging from approximately 100 µm (smaller) to occupying the entire ocular fundus quadrant (larger). Although lesions are generally regarded as nonprogressive and stationary, numerous studies have demonstrated slow, benign flat enlargement over time in up to 83% of patients, for which the percentage of area occupied by lacunae within the CHRPE is an important factor. [36] [2] The median rate of flat enlargement of solitary CHRPE is around 10 µm/month. [2] In addition, 32% of cases also show enlargement of lacunae in size or number over time. [2] Intraretinal focal pigmentation may be noted near the margin of solitary CHRPE. Lesions are often found incidentally during a routine eye examination, and patients are rarely symptomatic. Visual field defects corresponding to the area of the lesion are possible; however, lesions are generally located peripherally and are unlikely to be noticeable.

retrieved_docs.title: Humphrey Visual Field -- Procedures -- Visual Field
retrieved_docs.content: Thomas Young gave the extent of a uniocular visual field. [2] This consists of the central 30° field and extends to 60° superiorly and medially. The temporal extension is up to 100°, and the inferior extent is up to 80°. Binocular visual fields extend temporally to 200° with a central overlap of 120°. Edme Mariotte was the first to report that the physiologic blind spot corresponds to the location of the optic disc. [2] The blind spot is located between 10° to 20° temporal to the point of fixation. The physiological blind spot due to the optic disc is called the 'punctum caecum' or 'Mariotte's spot.' [6] A "scotoma" refers to a localized area of vision loss within the visual field, essentially a "blind spot." In contrast, a "field defect" is a broader term encompassing any loss of vision in a part of the visual field, which could include multiple scotomas or a larger area of reduced vision across the field; essentially, a scotoma is a specific type of field defect where vision loss is concentrated in a small, defined area.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypertrophy of Retinal Pigment Epithelium -- History and Physical -- Grouped or Multifocal Congenital Hypertrophy of the Retinal Pigmented Epithelium and Grouped Pigmentation of the Retina
retrieved_docs.content: Grouped pigmentation of the retina (GPR) resembles animal footprints or bear tracks. GPR appears similar to solitary CHRPE but presents unilaterally with 3 to 30 discreet lesions of various sizes (see Image. Bear Track Pigmented Lesions). These lesions are typically flat, round, or oval black spots with well-defined margins arranged in clusters. GPR lesions are generally smaller than unilateral CHRPEs, ranging from 0.1 to 0.3 mm in diameter, and confined to a single quadrant in the retina. Peripheral lesions are usually larger than posterior lesions and seem to radiate from the optic disc along the lines of Blashko lines or to proceed toward the retinal periphery. [37] Unlike solitary CHRPE, GPR lesions typically lack surrounding haloes and lacunae. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypertrophy of Retinal Pigment Epithelium -- Evaluation -- Dilated Fundus Examination
retrieved_docs.content: Detecting CHRPE lesions often requires evaluating the retinal periphery through dilated examination or nonmydriatic retinal imaging, as they are rarely found in the posterior pole. One or both procedures are virtually always performed as part of a comprehensive eye exam. Most biomicroscopes are equipped with a red-free filter that may aid in distinguishing between choroidal nevi and CHRPE lesions. Nevi disappears with red-free light, whereas CHRPE remains visible. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Ocular Ischemic Syndrome -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: Posterior segment manifestations are narrowed retinal arteries (90%), dilated and not tortuous retinal veins (90%), retinal hemorrhages (80%), microaneurysms (80%), retinal telangiectasia, cherry-red spot (12%), cholesterol emboli, glaucoma (neovascular glaucoma, normal-tension glaucoma), neovascularization (optic disc 35%, retina 8%), cotton-wool spots (6%), vitreous hemorrhage (4%), spontaneous retinal arteries pulsations (4%), anterior and posterior ischemic optic neuropathy (2%), choroidal neovascular membrane, areas of chorioretinal atrophy. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Humphrey Visual Field -- Clinical Significance -- Results of Humphrey Field Analyzer in Various Clinical Scenarios
retrieved_docs.content: Idiopathic Intracranial Hypertension: The field defects commonly found in these patients are enlargement of the blind spot, generalized constriction of the visual field, loss of inferonasal visual field, or nasal step. [52] Papilledema: Enlargement of the blind spot is the most commonly seen visual field defect, with constriction of the visual field seen in late cases where optic atrophy has started. [49] [53]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Disc Drusen -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Typically overall visual morbidity is minimal. Nerve drusen can be followed periodically and do not typically cause visual acuity loss. However, visual field loss is more common, with some estimates stating as many as 50% of children and nearly 90% of adults may have visual field defects, most commonly enlarged blind spots and peripheral constriction, or less commonly arcuate defects. More severe visual morbidity may arise from rare complications associated with nerve drusen, such as ischemic optic neuropathy. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Electron microscopy reveals whorled deposits within the lysosome, which have a multilayered appearance like a cut section of an onion. [22] In CRAO, the early changes are ischemic changes and swelling of the inner retina, which is followed by thinning of inner retinal layers with time. [24]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypertrophy of Retinal Pigment Epithelium -- Evaluation -- Visual Field Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: Although patients can manifest a visual field defect corresponding to the area of the CHRPE, they are unlikely to be detected through visual field analysis. This is because most lesions are situated more peripherally than the testing area covered by Humphrey visual field analyzers. The depth of the defect (absolute versus relative) usually correlates with lesion size. However, some studies suggest that younger patients typically manifest relative visual field defects, while older patients manifest absolute defects. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Atrophy -- Evaluation -- Visual Field Tests
retrieved_docs.content: These should be performed whenever possible to help in diagnosis as well as follow-up of the patient’s condition. The 30-2 program is most useful in the investigation of optic atrophy. Visual field changes can include enlargement of the blind spot and paracentral scotoma, altitudinal defects (as seen in AION and optic neuritis), and bitemporal defects (seen in compressive lesions, similar to optic chiasma tumors).

retrieved_docs.title: Cherry Red Spot -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: GM1 gangliosidosis type 1 (Generalized gangliosidosis or Landing disease) results from the deficiency of beta-galactosidase at chromosome 3p22.3. A cherry-red spot in the macula occurs in around 50% of cases. [11] There are three variants: Type I, or infantile form, is characterized by a severe disease with onset within the first year of life. Facial features are coarse with gingival hypertrophy. Other findings include developmental regression, increased startle response, skeletal anomalies, hepatosplenomegaly, intellectual disability, seizures, a cherry-red spot at the macula, corneal clouding, and death within early childhood. Type II or the late infantile or juvenile form presents within 18 months or five years, respectively. The severity is less than type I, and the cherry-red spot at the macula, visceromegaly, or facial features like type I are usually not seen. Type III or adult or chronic form is the mildest form. Features include dystonia and vertebral anomaly. The age of onset and life expectancy may be variable.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypertrophy of Retinal Pigment Epithelium -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Grouped or multifocal : These lesions are usually unilateral and appear similar to CHRPE but are multifocal. Grouped CHRPE lesions are clustered within a quadrant of the retina, often referred to as "bear tracks." They are congenital and benign, with no associated systemic conditions. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Hypertrophy of Retinal Pigment Epithelium -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Solitary CHRPE and GPR do not require treatment and can be monitored periodically. Solitary lesions should undergo close examination to detect rare nodule development and associated complications or malignant transformation (Please refer to the Prognosis section for more information). The lesion's dimensions can be measured, and fundus photography can be utilized to monitor changes in size and characteristics.

retrieved_docs.title: Optic Disc Drusen -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Typically, patients are asymptomatic, and the drusen are noticed incidentally on examination. However, up to 10% of patients may report transient visual obscurations, and up to 25% may present with visual field defects, including enlarged blind spots.

retrieved_docs.title: Hypertensive Retinopathy -- History and Physical -- Optic Nerve Changes
retrieved_docs.content: Wong et al identified several retinal signs associated with an increased risk of stroke. These signs include arteriovenous nicking, focal arteriolar narrowing (linked to arteriosclerosis), microaneurysms, cotton wool spots, retinal hemorrhages (both dot-blot and flame-shaped), and a decreased arteriovenous ratio. [52] The presence of arteriovenous nicking may indicate longstanding hypertension even when current blood pressure readings are normal. However, mild arteriovenous changes may be nonspecific and may occur in the absence of hypertension.

retrieved_docs.content: and retina, leading to scarring, atrophy, detachment, neovascularization and visual impairment. Accurate identification of the specific choroidal and retinal pathology is essential for comprehensive evaluation, multidisciplinary management and optimal patient care.

Clinical assessment of disease severity, such as disease condition determination, uncontrolled, is interpreted by the general clinical state and is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, underscoring the importance of recognizing uncontrolled secondary diabetes mellitus in patient care.

The retina is also the primary site for retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and various other retinopathies. Therapeutic interventions targeting these lesions comprise destructive procedures on the posterior segment of the eye, categorized under “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid,” with radiation‑based destruction of localized retinal lesions (e.g., macular edema or tumors) as a further sub‑category. The disease داء ريفسام is likewise identified with retinal findings, underscoring the eye’s role in its manifestation. Clinical assessment employs the concept of “general clinical state” to interpret both biologic function failure and uncontrolled disease conditions; uncontrolled disease conditions are associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, highlighting the need for vigilant monitoring. Biologic function failure is linked to hepatic‑renal failure, indicating a critical organ‑system

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: and secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene. Uncontrolled disease condition, interpreted by the general clinical state, is associated with diabetes mellitus with complications, indicating heightened disease burden. Multiple microvascular complications are explicitly attributed to secondary diabetes mellitus: mild non‑proliferative retinopathy, moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy, non‑proliferative retinopathy, retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, and nephropathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus. The retina is the finding site for these retinopathies as well as for retinal detachments, defects, vascular occlusion, and the disease entity داء ريفسام. Ocular destructive interventions are organized hierarchically: “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid” is the broad category; its child “destructive procedure on posterior segment of eye” includes the specific procedure “destruction of localized lesion of retina (e.g., macular

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 1253
retrieved_docs.content: حصوي (calcific degeneration of choroidal or retinal tissue), تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة (choroidal atrophy) and حثل المشيمية حول الحُليلة، جزئي (partial sclerosis of the choroid surrounding the macula). Together, these entities illustrate the extensive range of inflammatory, vascular, hemorrhagic, degenerative, and structural pathologies classified under Disorders of choroid and retina, underscoring the need for precise diagnosis and targeted management to preserve visual function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: detachment of the eye (انفصال مشيمية العين) and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Peripheral involvement includes peripheral disseminated choroiditis with chorioretinitis and peripheral focal retinitis with retinochoroiditis. Across all entities, the choroidea (choroid) is the anatomical locus of the characteristic lesions, underscoring its central role in pathophysiology, clinical assessment, precise diagnosis, and tailored management of choroidal and retinal disorders.

All of the retinopathy entities—Mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, Moderate nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, Non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus, and Retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus—have the retina as their finding site. The relationship “finding_site_of retina retinal detachments; defects; vascular occlusion; and retinopathy” underscores that retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and retinopathy are manifestations located in the retina. The statement “has_finding_site retina داء ريفسام” further emphasizes that retinal disease (داء ريفسام) is identified at the retina. Therapeutic interventions targeting retinal lesions are organized hierarchically. “Destruction of localized lesion of retina (eg, macular edema, tumors), one or more sessions; radiation by implantation of source” is a child procedure of the broader category

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the primary anatomic site for a variety of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusions, and retinopathy. In patients with secondary diabetes mellitus, both non‑proliferative retinopathy and moderate non‑proliferative retinopathy are identified as retinal findings, underscoring that diabetic retinopathy manifests within the retinal tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to as داء ريفسام also presents its findings in the retina. Recognizing the retina as the common location for these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and management in ophthalmic and systemic disease care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 196
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of the choroid and retina (ICD‑10 H30‑H36) constitute a broad diagnostic group in which vascular, inflammatory, infectious, degenerative, atrophic, traumatic, postoperative and congenital pathologies converge on the choroidea (choroid) and the retina. Vascular complications include choroidal‑retinal neovascularization, superficial retinal hemorrhage, retinal flebitis, generalized retinal vascular changes, diabetic microvascular retinal disease (أم الدم المجهرية الشبكية السكرية), hypertensive retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية الناجم عن فَرْط ضَغْطِ الدَّم), proliferative diabetic retinopathy (اعْتِلاَلُ الشَّبَكِيَّةِ السكري التكاثري), cotton‑wool spots (بُقَعُ روث), spot hemorrhage (نزيف اللطخة الشبكي), retinal hemorrhage (نَزِيف في الشبكية), subretinal hemorrhage (نزيف مشيمي, نزيف مشيمي باثق, نَزِيف تحت الشبكية) and retinal artery or vein occlusion (خثار الشريان الشبكي, خثار الوريد الشبكي). Inflammatory and infectious entities that target the choroid comprise disseminated

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 228
retrieved_docs.content: without complication, with a further branch “secondary diabetes mellitus, without mention of complication” and a specialized form “secondary diabetes mellitus with diabetic peripheral angiopathy with gangrene.” Patients with secondary diabetes mellitus are predisposed to multiple microvascular complications. Mild, moderate, and general non‑proliferative retinopathy attributed to secondary diabetes mellitus, as well as broader retinopathy and nephropathy linked to the same disease, all arise from the underlying secondary diabetes mellitus and have the retina as the primary finding site. The retina also serves as the anatomical site for retinal detachments, retinal defects, vascular occlusions, and various other forms of retinopathy. In addition, the retina functions as a finding site for داء ريفسام, indicating that ocular examination of the retina can reveal manifestations of this disease and underscoring the importance of retinal assessment for accurate diagnosis and management of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 1073
retrieved_docs.content: (hemorrhagic choroidal detachment); حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي (hereditary choroidal cyst); and ندبة مشيمية شبكية (choroidal retinal scar). Together, these child entities demonstrate that disorders of the choroid and retina can present as vascular occlusions, inflammatory lesions, neovascular growth, scarring, dystrophies, detachments and hereditary abnormalities, underscoring the importance of precise classification for accurate diagnosis, targeted management and prognostication in ophthalmic care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: hierarchically. “Destruction of localized lesion of retina (eg, macular edema, tumors), one or more sessions; radiation by implantation of source” is a child procedure of the broader category “Destructive procedure on posterior segment of eye,” which itself is a parent of “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid.” The procedure “destruction of lesion of retina and choroid” has a direct procedure site of the retina, confirming that these destructive ocular treatments are directed at the same anatomical location affected by the diabetic retinopathies. In summary, secondary diabetes mellitus leads to retinal and renal complications, with the retina serving as the primary site of disease manifestation and therapeutic targeting. Understanding the hierarchical classification of secondary diabetes mellitus, its specific complications (various forms of retinopathy and nephropathy), and the relationship between these complications and ocular destructive procedures is essential for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: The retina serves as the anatomical site for a range of ocular pathologies, including retinal detachments, structural defects, vascular occlusion, and various forms of retinopathy. Both mild nonproliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus and non‑proliferative retinopathy due to secondary diabetes mellitus are identified on the retina, indicating that diabetic retinal changes are localized to this tissue. Additionally, the condition referred to in Arabic as داء ريفسام also has the retina as its finding site, further emphasizing the retina’s central role in ocular disease manifestation. Recognizing that these diverse abnormalities share the retina as their common location aids clinicians in targeted examination and management of retinal disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1158
retrieved_docs.content: choroid, while “litteken; choroidea” and retinal‑choroidal scar (“ندبة مشيمية شبكية”) denote isolated choroidal scar tissue. The choroidea also serves as the direct procedural site for therapeutic interventions aimed at destroying retinal and choroidal lesions, underscoring its dual role as both a target for lesion‑destruction procedures and a location where retinal‑choroidal scarring can be detected, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and monitoring of ocular diseases.
